text: Hello, my name is Kristen Bremer and I'm a general surgery resident at Creighton University School of Medicine in Omaha, Nebraska.
Today I will be presenting the technical aspects of robotic excision of a large epiphrenic diverticulum. We have no conflicts of interest.

An epiphrenic diverticulum is located in the distal esophagus above the diaphragm. These are typically caused by some type of esophageal motility disorder, which causes increased pressure on the wall of the esophagus, leading to diverticulum formation. They are false diverticula, more common on the right.

Our patient is a fifty-six-year-old male who reported regurgitation for the past twelve years, which was worse with laying on his left side. These symptoms had worsened over the past two years, with worsening regurgitation and the addition of dysphagia and recurrent aspiration.

The patient underwent barium swallow, which showed a large, wide-mouthed diverticulum, eleven point five centimeters in size and the greatest dimension. An EGD showed food and fluid in the esophagus with a wide-mouthed epiphrenic diverticulum and a tight lower esophageal sphincter. The second photo showed the sphincter on the left and the diverticulum on the right, indicated by the yellow arrow. The third photo is a view inside this diverticulum after the debris was cleared out with multiple passes of a Roth net. Manometry showed a normal lower esophageal sphincter mean IRP as well as distal esophageal spasm.

All options, including endoscopic management of the diverticulum, were discussed with the patient. The patient elected to undergo a robotic-assisted laparoscopic epiphrenic diverticulectomy with hellermyotomy and dora fundoplication.

The surgical team uses three 8mm robotic ports, a 12mm robotic port for the stapler, an 8mm assist port in the right lower quadrant, and a liver retractor. The initial approach involves mobilizing the right crus of the diaphragm and entering into the mediastinum.
The surgeon mobilizes the entire frenoesophageal membrane, followed by the mobilization of the mediastinum and distal esophagus up to eight centimeters.

The surgeon identifies the diverticulum adherent to the pleura on the right side. A barium swallow schematic in the left upper screen shows the dissection orientation. The surgeon performs a circumferential dissection of the diverticulum, starting from the distal aspect and moving anteriorly toward the pericardium. The pleura is gently peeled away from the diverticulum, and posterior dissection is also performed.

The surgeon uses endoscopy to visualize the diverticulum and its relation to surrounding structures. Blunt dissection techniques are employed to carefully separate and remove adhesions between the diverticulum and adjacent tissues.
The robotic-assisted camera provides stable visualization as the surgeon performs mediastinal dissection, focusing on removing adhesions from the proximal aspect of the diverticulum.
The surgeon completes the separation of the diverticulum from surrounding structures, reaching its superior extent. The diverticulum is then delivered out of the mediastinum.

After adhesiolysis, the surgeon identifies the neck of the diverticulum, which spans from five to nine centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction. The diverticulectomy is performed using multiple endo-GI blue loads, with an endoscope serving as a bougie. The stapled area is then imbricated using vicryl sutures.

The surgeon performs a myotomy on the anterolateral wall, with the diverticulum located in the anteromedial position. The myotomy extends two centimeters onto the stomach and five centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction. The crura is approximated using interrupted silk sutures, followed by an anterior door fundoplication.
Intraoperative endoscopy reveals a well-opened gastroesophageal junction with a visible fundoplication valve. The endoscopic view confirms the absence of mucostomy.
The surgeon removes the divided diverticulum using an endobag, completing the surgical procedure.

In conclusion, a large epiphrenic diverticulectomy can be performed safely using a transabdominal approach. Visualization within the mediastinum has significantly improved with utilization of a robotic platform. Thank you.
